steps.title: Find the shared duty
steps.content: Defoe describes two responsibilities for historians, both about how a story is supported. When a story is backed by sufficient authority, the historian should give the proper voucher or source. When a story lacks sufficient authority, the historian should say so. The repeated idea is that historians must present facts with their legitimate authority and make the level of support clear to readers.

steps.title: Rule out overstatements
steps.content: Choice A claims no memories can be trusted and that records may be false, but Defoe only discusses whether authority is sufficient. Choice B says historians should quote only widely credible figures, while the passage allows weaker material if it is labeled. Choice C invents difficulty, inaccuracy, and remorse. Choice D states the central principle from both halves of the passage: historians should make clear how trustworthy each source is. The answer is D.
